title: Vendor c/common pasta branch for testing
body:
Test swapping the default from Slirp4netns to Pasta

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
Connectivity for rootless containers is now provided by Pasta by default, instead of slirp4netns
```

comments.body: Cockpit tests failed for commit 27f523cffd270b15f8360f1b13a20d9329c35b62. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Cockpit tests failed for commit 7a5015dde4cdfd595b210f430a15ae91c4d3031e. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Looks like I broke remote, but everything else seems solid
Cockpit tests failed for commit c70cd90c32d38e08ae1b4e4d524f127436a583e8. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
I'm actually not sure how this APIv2 test passed in the first place. We don't populate the NetworkSettings.Networks field for slirp4netns either per my testing.
see https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/21651706e3277cbf4d646f7f5eddcf2d9e0e25b6

Seems like the test failures are no mostly things that expect slirp4netns default so we need to change them, the timeouts in the sys tests are however very concerning because they do not seem to hang at a specific point and just are much slower.
Cockpit tests failed for commit a31c715cc7aebdd5a1b444719da8bb58973bb707. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Cockpit tests failed for commit ae3891bb8e9a8efd8ffbd6f56fc2a66f62af5a7d. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Cockpit tests failed for commit c0a6d5ad7839e141952cc55ad888c57266f663c8. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Cockpit tests failed for commit 67e07cd091c975d2f5e2a917ae1cf5fe03c8f6e8. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
APIv2 failure is legitimate, `getContainerNetIO` needs a rewrite to handle Pasta properly.
System tests are hard-stuck on `[+2306s] ok 647 [700] podman kube play healthcheck should wait initialDelaySeconds before updating status (healthy)` (or, more accurately, whatever comes after this)
I see nothing in there that Pasta ought to have broken...
Odd, can you reproduce locally?
The 700-play file passes locally without issue (and don't seem to take unduly long either)
If nothing helps there is this re-run with terminal access feature in the cirrus web ui. It should allow you to look around while the tests are running.
Cockpit tests failed for commit ab6f00aed185cf65feaf5aec14b72c8a5081a157. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.

On rerun, failed at `[+2330s] ok 645 [700] podman kube generate tmpfs on /tmp`

Same file, different test?
Cockpit tests failed for commit 21cdf07c60041c4584dfeb189ed7d837ecfabdb6. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.

The [cockpit-podman failure](https://artifacts.dev.testing-farm.io/60f22ae3-c78c-4f72-b2b7-2cd0128fbf74/) at least warrants some discussion:
```
wait_js_cond(ph_in_text(".pf-v5-c-alert","address already in use")): actual text: Danger alert:Container failed to be started Error message: Internal Server Error: pasta failed with exit code 1:
No external routable interface for IPv6
Failed to bind any port for '-t 5000-5000:5000-5000', exiting
```
This is indeed a test that tries to run two containers on the same port, which should fail with "address already in use". Showing "pasta failed with exit code 1" is not particularly useful, and "No external routable interface for IPv6" is outright confusing as none of this has anything to do with IPv6. "failed to bind any port" is vaguely related, but "address already in use" was indeed a so much better error message, i.e. `strerror(EADDRINUSE)`. Could this be put back?

> The [cockpit-podman failure](https://artifacts.dev.testing-farm.io/60f22ae3-c78c-4f72-b2b7-2cd0128fbf74/) at least warrants some discussion:
> 
> ```
> wait_js_cond(ph_in_text(".pf-v5-c-alert","address already in use")): actual text: Danger alert:Container failed to be started Error message: Internal Server Error: pasta failed with exit code 1:
> No external routable interface for IPv6
> Failed to bind any port for '-t 5000-5000:5000-5000', exiting
> ```
> 
> This is indeed a test that tries to run two containers on the same port, which should fail with "address already in use". Showing "pasta failed with exit code 1" is not particularly useful, and "No external routable interface for IPv6" is outright confusing as none of this has anything to do with IPv6. "failed to bind any port" is vaguely related, but "address already in use" was indeed a so much better error message, i.e. `strerror(EADDRINUSE)`. Could this be put back?

100% agree as far as the bind error goes, I already reported this to the pasta devs together with another "bug": https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/ef7dbce7-bde4-4925-81d4-acbf783f62c5@redhat.com

For the `No external routable interface for IPv6` message this will always be shown by pasta if you do not have public ipv6 address. Podman however only outputs the messages on an pasta exit > 0 so in a non error case you never see this.
I think it would be great to not show this every time from a podman users and also pasta users on the cli but pasta maintainers might think otherwise. One way to silence this is by passing `-4` as arg but this doesn't really make sense for us in podman as we cannot really know if ipv6 is working or not without adding a lot of extra code to check in the same way pasta does.

We're leaking Pasta processes. ~10-15 by my observation by completion of the system test suite. Closing them causes the suite to complete successfully.

No evidence of anything that is not Pasta leaking. No excess Conmons, no excess container processes, no excess Podmans. Seems like a legitimate bug (potentially in Pasta?). I don't know if it replicates outside CI, but I've been playing with Pasta for most of a week without rebooting (including several runs of the offending system tests) and I have no leaks on my system.
Cockpit tests failed for commit c6a3e0c578ed5cf7b6da74d11374440eca32f2ba. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Cockpit tests failed for commit d15c903540672d0754e34bbb070e16d31520a2f0. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Ephemeral COPR build failed. @containers/packit-build please check.
We leak 5 pasta processes in `podman pull image with additional store` which only does `podman images` and `podman pull`. So there's something very wrong here.

> For the `No external routable interface for IPv6` message this will always be shown by pasta if you do not have public ipv6 address. Podman however only outputs the messages on an pasta exit > 0 so in a non error case you never see this. I think it would be great to not show this every time from a podman users and also pasta users on the cli but pasta maintainers might think otherwise. One way to silence this is by passing `-4` as arg but this doesn't really make sense for us in podman as we cannot really know if ipv6 is working or not without adding a lot of extra code to check in the same way pasta does.

The fact is that pasta is configured to use IPv6 (because that's the default and it's not disabled), but IPv6 is not available. We could reasonably change that message to have informational severity (currently it's a warning) and then pass `-q` / `--quiet`, so that informational messages are not logged. Would that help? Is there any other preference? We can also change the message if somebody has a specific suggestion.

> We're leaking Pasta processes. ~10-15 by my observation by completion of the system test suite. Closing them causes the suite to complete successfully.
> 
> No evidence of anything that is not Pasta leaking. No excess Conmons, no excess container processes, no excess Podmans. Seems like a legitimate bug (potentially in Pasta?). I don't know if it replicates outside CI, but I've been playing with Pasta for most of a week without rebooting (including several runs of the offending system tests) and I have no leaks on my system.

The mechanism pasta uses to terminate itself when the container goes away is an inotify monitor on the filesystem-bound network namespace. As long as something else uses the namespace (some kind of debug tracing in the CI?), the network namespace is referenced and the kernel won't delete it. Can you check if that path (`/run/user/.../netns/...`, matching the `--netns` parameter on pasta's command line) still exists?

> > We're leaking Pasta processes. ~10-15 by my observation by completion of the system test suite. Closing them causes the suite to complete successfully.
> >
> > No evidence of anything that is not Pasta leaking. No excess Conmons, no excess container processes, no excess Podmans. Seems like a legitimate bug (potentially in Pasta?). I don't know if it replicates outside CI, but I've been playing with Pasta for most of a week without rebooting (including several runs of the offending system tests) and I have no leaks on my system.
> >
> The mechanism pasta uses to terminate itself when the container goes away is an inotify monitor on the filesystem-bound network namespace. As long as something else uses the namespace (some kind of debug tracing in the CI?), the network namespace is referenced and the kernel won't delete it. Can you check if that path (/run/user/.../netns/..., matching the --netns parameter on pasta's command line) still exists?

I wonder if that might have anything to do with https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/21569 (even though that appears as the opposite problem).

@sbrivio-rh I am very confident that we do not leak the netns path. The code is the same for all network cleanup paths and while there were possible leaks in the past. I don't think we are actually leaking them currently here, at least not in a such big fashion.

What I find much more likely is that we run out of inotify watchers (we had similar problems in the past) and reading pasta code seems to suggest that this is not a fatal error and just logged to stderr which podman hides currently.





> > For the `No external routable interface for IPv6` message this will always be shown by pasta if you do not have public ipv6 address. Podman however only outputs the messages on an pasta exit > 0 so in a non error case you never see this. I think it would be great to not show this every time from a podman users and also pasta users on the cli but pasta maintainers might think otherwise. One way to silence this is by passing `-4` as arg but this doesn't really make sense for us in podman as we cannot really know if ipv6 is working or not without adding a lot of extra code to check in the same way pasta does.
> 
> The fact is that pasta is configured to use IPv6 (because that's the default and it's not disabled), but IPv6 is not available. We could reasonably change that message to have informational severity (currently it's a warning) and then pass `-q` / `--quiet`, so that informational messages are not logged. Would that help? Is there any other preference? We can also change the message if somebody has a specific suggestion.

Passing --quiet sounds reasonable to me if that means we will we still see actually warning and errors. So as long as we can be sure that a default configuration does not output anything unless there were actually any problems then I think we should change podman to always log the pasta output (if there was any) that should give us the ability to actually log non fatal error from pasta to the users.

> @sbrivio-rh I am very confident that we do not leak the netns path. The code is the same for all network cleanup paths and while there were possible leaks in the past. I don't think we are actually leaking them currently here, at least not in a such big fashion.
> 
> What I find much more likely is that we run out of inotify watchers (we had similar problems in the past) and reading pasta code seems to suggest that this is not a fatal error and just logged to stderr which podman hides currently.

Oops, yes, that's two bugs in one: 1. I used plain `perror()` there for no particular reason, so the error is just sent to `stderr` but not logged, and 2. we don't fail on that.

For 1., the fix is obvious.

For 2., we could implement a periodic timer as a fall-back mechanism, that should be trivial as well. We don't have access to the filesystem after the initial configuration phase, but I think grabbing a handle on the parent directory will do.

I plan to post both changes in a bit.

> > > For the `No external routable interface for IPv6` message this will always be shown by pasta if you do not have public ipv6 address. Podman however only outputs the messages on an pasta exit > 0 so in a non error case you never see this. I think it would be great to not show this every time from a podman users and also pasta users on the cli but pasta maintainers might think otherwise. One way to silence this is by passing `-4` as arg but this doesn't really make sense for us in podman as we cannot really know if ipv6 is working or not without adding a lot of extra code to check in the same way pasta does.
> > 
> > 
> > The fact is that pasta is configured to use IPv6 (because that's the default and it's not disabled), but IPv6 is not available. We could reasonably change that message to have informational severity (currently it's a warning) and then pass `-q` / `--quiet`, so that informational messages are not logged. Would that help? Is there any other preference? We can also change the message if somebody has a specific suggestion.
> 
> Passing --quiet sounds reasonable to me if that means we will we still see actually warning and errors. So as long as we can be sure that a default configuration does not output anything unless there were actually any problems then I think we should change podman to always log the pasta output (if there was any) that should give us the ability to actually log non fatal error from pasta to the users.

At the moment, `--quiet` would hide warnings as well. But with pasta, unless you enable debug messages, informational messages are not displayed anyway. I think we already have the correct behaviour, I just need to turn that message into an informational one. I'll post a patch for this as well.

> At the moment, --quiet would hide warnings as well. But with pasta, unless you enable debug messages, informational messages are not displayed anyway. I think we already have the correct behaviour, I just need to turn that message into an informational one. I'll post a patch for this as well.

Yes this sounds good, I looked at all the warn()/err() calls in passt/pasta and they look reasonable, so if we get rid off the ipv6 warning (I guess the same guess for the ipv4 one if we assume and ipv6 only setup) then I think podman should show the pasta warnings by default because something like `--no-copy-addrs will be dropped soon` should definitely reach it's way to end users so they are not surprised by this. And then the end users has the option to pass --quiet themselves if they do not want these warnings.
Cockpit tests failed for commit 4aa75a130d8d3ddb63021cbd776941d1822ed081. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
At this point, I'm wondering why Pasta is being called at all. We're seeing leaks from tests that do not run any containers.
We should have a better idea once the latest round of tests finishes
Ephemeral COPR build failed. @containers/packit-build please check.
Cockpit tests failed for commit 322eacb1595fb1c9438199cdf5ba0f465538c553. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Ephemeral COPR build failed. @containers/packit-build please check.
The net namespaces do seem to still exist, though most of them are /proc/$PID/ns/net instead of a netns we created.
One of the leaks seems to be a registry container. @edsantiago Any chance those are wired specially in the tests, wouldn't be removed by cleanup?

Unlikely? https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/bbd4476d310aba32e796d0177c39039759671b57/test/system/setup_suite.bash#L36-L39


 I think we would've noticed by now if that were not running.

We're definitely leaking a registry. Does not seem to be containerized, though? If it is, the associated conmon died.

Unfortunately that's only 1 of our many leaked pastas.

@Luap99 @sbrivio-rh What would happen if Pasta was started pointed to a `/proc/$PID/ns/net` that was just the root network namespace? `lsns` is only finding PID 1's network namespace, so I'm starting to think we're mistakenly starting pasta for host-network processes.

> @Luap99 @sbrivio-rh What would happen if Pasta was started pointed to a `/proc/$PID/ns/net` that was just the root network namespace?

It would tell you nope, I can't do anything there. In other words,
```
$ pasta --netns-only 1
Couldn't open user namespace /proc/1/ns/user: Permission denied
```

> > @Luap99 @sbrivio-rh What would happen if Pasta was started pointed to a `/proc/$PID/ns/net` that was just the root network namespace?
> 
> It would tell you nope, I can't do anything there. In other words,
> 
> ```
> $ pasta --netns-only 1
> Couldn't open user namespace /proc/1/ns/user: Permission denied
> ```

yes a rootless user cannot never setns on the host namespace even if it is already part of the hostns.

Mistake on my part, thought Pasta joined the container netns, it's actually listed as being in the root netns per /proc/.

Given what I'm seeing, I tend to agree that this is probably an inotify issue. We'll probably need a package with that patch to test with?
There are no active nsfs mounts on the system and still ~20 pasta processes. I think that confirms an inotify leak.

> Mistake on my part, thought Pasta joined the container netns, it's actually listed as being in the root netns per /proc/.
> 
> Given what I'm seeing, I tend to agree that this is probably an inotify issue. We'll probably need a package with that patch to test with?

Maybe patch c/common pasta setup logic to look for the string `inotify` on stderr, then just print the full stderr and panic then we know if inotify is the root cause.
Not a single test failed with the panic added, nor was a single panic printed.

> Not a single test failed with the panic added, nor was a single panic printed.

Wait a moment with that (quoting) hooray emoji: if no messages weren't printed, we didn't catch the error, right? I wonder, though, shouldn't this `	if strings.Contains(string(out), "inotify") { ` be `	if strings.Contains(string(err), "inotify") {` instead? Because pasta is printing that stuff on stderr, not stdout.
`CombinedOutput()` is stdout + stderr combined, so it should be in there

> `CombinedOutput()` is stdout + stderr combined, so it should be in there

Right, I see now. Then I have no idea why catching stderr hides the issue (that's pretty much the symptom, right?)

Current theory: The panics are happening, but are not being logged, because the Podman processes launching Pasta are somewhere outside of the tests themselves. Registry containers, maybe? We're leaking Pasta processes from tests that do not launch a single container; something else in the suite must be creating them, and whatever it is, it may not be set up to log to terminal.
Try [this](https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2024-7bb72e9605), it's good stuff.

Ok I think I finally understand the problem here.
`podman build` (buildah) is leaking the pasta processes. The main difference between the buildah and podman code is how we hand over the netns. Podman always used a bind mounted netns, i.e. `/run/user/100/netns/netns-xxx`, while the buildah code always passes down `/proc/$PIDofContainerProcess/ns/net`.

The short version is basically that inotify on /proc does not seem to work, `inotify(7)` says

> Furthermore, various pseudo-filesystems such as /proc, /sys, and
       /dev/pts are not monitorable with inotify.


Here is a pure pasta reproducer in case it is needed (I tested with the latest inotify patch from @sbrivio-rh), the interesting part is the registering inotiy works successfully otherwise pasta would log the problems so there is no reason for pasta to think it should fall back to the timer.
```
$ unshare -r
$ unshare -n sleep 1000 &
[1] 22806
$ ./pasta --config-net --netns /proc/$!/ns/net
No routable interface for IPv6: IPv6 is disabled
Template interface: enp9s0u2u1u2 (IPv4)
Namespace interface: enp9s0u2u1u2
MAC:
    host: 08:3a:88:5b:5a:71
DHCP:
    assign: 192.168.188.25
    mask: 255.255.255.0
    router: 192.168.188.1
DNS:
    192.168.188.1
DNS search list:
    fritz.box
$ nsenter --net=/proc/$!/ns/net ip a
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host proto kernel_lo 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: enp9s0u2u1u2: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65520 qdisc fq_codel state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/ether fa:cf:5d:50:e1:36 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 192.168.188.25/24 brd 192.168.188.255 scope global noprefixroute enp9s0u2u1u2
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::f8cf:5dff:fe50:e136/64 scope link proto kernel_ll 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

$ pidof pasta
22947
$ kill -9 22806
[1]+  Killed                  unshare -n sleep 1000
$ pidof pasta
22947
```


That gives us some option we consider to fix this:
a) always bind mount the netns even in buildah (it really shouldn't be needed as it just adds extra logic and the main issue is the buildah has no state so if you kill the process it will leak the netns mount forever which is bad)
b) teach pasta to parse the special /proc/$pid/ns/net path and get the pid from there, in this case it is possible to open a pidfd and this one can be used with epoll to detect the exit. I think this only works on more modern kernel I haven't check the exact requirements so not sure if this would be blocker and we need a solution for older kernels
c) always use the timer approach if the detect the /proc path.

> Ok I think I finally understand the problem here. `podman build` (buildah) is leaking the pasta processes. The main difference between the buildah and podman code is how we hand over the netns. Podman always used a bind mounted netns, i.e. `/run/user/100/netns/netns-xxx`, while the buildah code always passes down `/proc/$PIDofContainerProcess/ns/net`.

Uh-oh, I had no idea buildah did that.

> The short version is basically that inotify on /proc does not seem to work, `inotify(7)` says
> 
> > Furthermore, various pseudo-filesystems such as /proc, /sys, and
> > /dev/pts are not monitorable with inotify.

Sure... actually, we shouldn't even try to set up the inotify watch, but it's harmless.

> [...]
>
> That gives us some option we consider to fix this: a) always bind mount the netns even in buildah (it really shouldn't be needed as it just adds extra logic and the main issue is the buildah has no state so if you kill the process it will leak the netns mount forever which is bad)

Right, yes, this sounds bad.

> b) teach pasta to parse the special /proc/$pid/ns/net path and get the pid from there, in this case it is possible to open a pidfd and this one can be used with epoll to detect the exit. I think this only works on more modern kernel I haven't check the exact requirements so not sure if this would be blocker and we need a solution for older kernels

What happens if a third process (not buildah, not pasta) is now using that network namespace? Do you expect pasta to terminate once the buildah process stops using that network namespace, or should all the related processes terminate?

To watch for processes using the same network namespace, we can recycle something like `pasta_ns_cleanup()` we had before commit 0515adceaa8f ("passt, pasta: Namespace-based sandboxing, defer seccomp policy application").

> c) always use the timer approach if the detect the /proc path.

I'm not really fond of this. We can also implement b) with the netlink "connector" implementation:

```
$ git describe 9f46080c41d5f3f7c00b4e169ba4b0b2865258bf
v2.6.14-2623-g9f46080c41d5
```

> > Ok I think I finally understand the problem here. `podman build` (buildah) is leaking the pasta processes. The main difference between the buildah and podman code is how we hand over the netns. Podman always used a bind mounted netns, i.e. `/run/user/100/netns/netns-xxx`, while the buildah code always passes down `/proc/$PIDofContainerProcess/ns/net`.
> 
> Uh-oh, I had no idea buildah did that.

Yeah that is the reason I send https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20230623082531.25947-2-pholzing@redhat.com/ because otherwise it wouldn't even work. 




> > b) teach pasta to parse the special /proc/$pid/ns/net path and get the pid from there, in this case it is possible to open a pidfd and this one can be used with epoll to detect the exit. I think this only works on more modern kernel I haven't check the exact requirements so not sure if this would be blocker and we need a solution for older kernels
> 
> What happens if a third process (not buildah, not pasta) is now using that network namespace? Do you expect pasta to terminate once the buildah process stops using that network namespace, or should all the related processes terminate?

It should exit when the main process exits not wait for all processes IMO, just like it exits when we delete the netns bind mount and does not care if any processes are still running in the netns. But it shouldn't really matter for us, if users start attaching other processes the these namespaces created by us then this is really undefined behaviour already.

> 
> To watch for processes using the same network namespace, we can recycle something like `pasta_ns_cleanup()` we had before commit 0515adceaa8f ("passt, pasta: Namespace-based sandboxing, defer seccomp policy application").

I don't think something like this is needed or should be done, I would not kill other processes as we cannot have any knowledge on what they are doing. I would just call it a user problem if they attach pasta to one pid but then join other process to it and then terminate the main process with out the others. In our general use cases we use pid namesapces anyway so the main process is pid 1 in the ns which means when it exits all the child processes will be terminated anyway.

> > To watch for processes using the same network namespace, we can recycle something like `pasta_ns_cleanup()` we had before commit 0515adceaa8f ("passt, pasta: Namespace-based sandboxing, defer seccomp policy application").
> 
> I don't think something like this is needed or should be done, I would not kill other processes as we cannot have any knowledge on what they are doing.

Sure, we shouldn't kill other processes -- I mentioned that implementation for the part that checks which processes are using a given network namespace, nothing else.

> I would just call it a user problem if they attach pasta to one pid but then join other process to it and then terminate the main process with out the others. In our general use cases we use pid namesapces anyway so the main process is pid 1 in the ns which means when it exits all the child processes will be terminated anyway.

...but not with Buildah, right? Because if pasta could be started from a throw-away PID namespace, then we wouldn't need to care at all.

Anyway, I'm pondering a few different ways to deal with this. Ideally I would like to get as close as possible as what the kernel does to decide that a namespace can be reaped: if pasta is the only process using that namespace, that's the most direct indication that it doesn't need to run anymore. But I couldn't find any obvious way to get a notifiable reference count for a namespace node from procfs, yet.

The netlink connector interface paired with some procfs scanning would work, but it's clunky.

As you mentioned, `pidfd_open()` is perhaps too recent (5.3), but if we have a robust (and small) fallback mechanism for older kernels we could consider it. On the other hand it doesn't quite reflect the reference count concept that I'd like to have.

> > I would just call it a user problem if they attach pasta to one pid but then join other process to it and then terminate the main process with out the others. In our general use cases we use pid namesapces anyway so the main process is pid 1 in the ns which means when it exits all the child processes will be terminated anyway.
> 
> ...but not with Buildah, right? Because if pasta could be started from a throw-away PID namespace, then we wouldn't need to care at all.

No pasta is never launched from within the pidns by us. I just mean all the other processes that will be part of the netns will be a child of the main process and part of that pidns so we do not really need to worry about them, tracking the one pid I hand over in the /proc path should really do it for us at least.


> Anyway, I'm pondering a few different ways to deal with this. Ideally I would like to get as close as possible as what the kernel does to decide that a namespace can be reaped: if pasta is the only process using that namespace, that's the most direct indication that it doesn't need to run anymore. But I couldn't find any obvious way to get a notifiable reference count for a namespace node from procfs, yet.

This will not work with podman unless you can account for the fact that we launch pasta before the container process in podman so there will be no other process in this namespace for a short period, the only other reference is the active bind mount beside the pasta process itself. So you would have to check /proc + all netns bind mounts. But then again if pasta is part of a different mountns then it can get this wrong as pasta might not see all the netns mounts, same for pidns and checking /proc I would assume. I don't think these would be real problems for podman/buildah but I just mentioned them because I do not think you can really check this from the userspace considering that the namespaces can hide the information you want.

> > c) always use the timer approach if the detect the /proc path.
> 
> I'm not really fond of this.

...but it looks like we have to do that anyway, patch at https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20240217133457.2331118-1-sbrivio@redhat.com/T/#u, because:

> > b) teach pasta to parse the special /proc/$pid/ns/net path and get the pid from there, in this case it is possible to open a pidfd and this one can be used with epoll to detect the exit. I think this only works on more modern kernel I haven't check the exact requirements so not sure if this would be blocker and we need a solution for older kernels

...even if we do this, the PID is passed on the command line, and doesn't necessarily represent a process that still exists or is still the same as we parse the command line, even though that might be always the case for Buildah.

There might be ways to actually guarantee that, but another reason why I started preferring c) is that pretty much anything else needs further system calls to be enabled in the seccomp profile, whereas the timer I just introduced uses `openat()`, which we needed anyway. We already have 41 system calls allowed for pasta on x86_64 and it's way too many.

I will look into on Monday, the odd thing is that the netns handling code hasn't changed at all. pasta/slirp4netns the codde should work the same so why does the error pop up now?

Ok I think I see the issue but not why it is pasta specific, the registry is started at some point earlier in the test and only stopped at a later point. All podman commands are part of the same userns normally but at one point later in the test we run podman system migrate which also kills the userns pause pid process so the userns is no longer accessible to podman. The next podman command creates a new one and all podman commands work fine. However at the end if we try to stop the registry container that was created in the previous userns and because we are in different userns/mountns we cannot see the netns mount and fail to unmount.

https://github.com/containers/common/pull/1872 should help although we should also fix this in the tests here:
```diff
diff --git a/test/system/550-pause-process.bats b/test/system/550-pause-process.bats
index 4e99d85f2..9bf6e74a0 100644
--- a/test/system/550-pause-process.bats
+++ b/test/system/550-pause-process.bats
@@ -6,6 +6,15 @@
 load helpers
 load helpers.sig-proxy
 
+function setup() {
+    # We have to stop the background registry here. These tests kill the podman pause
+    # process which means commands after that are in a new one and when the cleanup
+    # later tries to stop the registry container it will be in the wrong ns and can fail.
+    # https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/21563#issuecomment-1960047648
+    stop_registry
+    basic_setup
+}
+
 function _check_pause_process() {
     pause_pid=
     if [[ -z "$pause_pid_file" ]]; then
```

I think the diff should make it work here.
Nice. Quick suggestion: [setup_file()](https://bats-core.readthedocs.io/en/stable/writing-tests.html#setup-and-teardown-pre-and-post-test-hooks) instead of `setup()`

like this then?
```diff
diff --git a/test/system/550-pause-process.bats b/test/system/550-pause-process.bats
index 4e99d85f2..599f54fb9 100644
--- a/test/system/550-pause-process.bats
+++ b/test/system/550-pause-process.bats
@@ -6,6 +6,14 @@
 load helpers
 load helpers.sig-proxy
 
+function setup_file() {
+    # We have to stop the background registry here. These tests kill the podman pause
+    # process which means commands after that are in a new one and when the cleanup
+    # later tries to stop the registry container it will be in the wrong ns and can fail.
+    # https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/21563#issuecomment-1960047648
+    stop_registry
+}
+
 function _check_pause_process() {
     pause_pid=
     if [[ -z "$pause_pid_file" ]]; then
```
